claims.text: The GAA c.1935C>A (p.Asp645Glu) variant is described as the most frequent pathogenic variant associated with infantile-onset Pompe disease (IOPD) in Southern Chinese, Taiwanese, and Southeast Asian populations of Han ancestry, representing 36–80% of mutations in Taiwanese IOPD patients.

citations.quote: the GAA c.1935C>A transversion in exon 14, which results in the p.Asp645Glu (p.D645E) missense mutation, is the most frequent pathogenic variant associated with IOPD in the Southern Chinese, Taiwanese, and Southeast Asian populations of Han ancestry, but is not frequently reported in any other region
In Taiwanese populations, this c.1935C>A variant represents 36–80% of mutations

claims.text: The GAA c.1935C>A variant is explicitly classified as a pathogenic mutation causing infantile-onset Pompe disease (IOPD), with neonatal onset and severe phenotype including hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, profound muscle hypotonia, respiratory failure, and infantile mortality.

citations.quote: The c.1935C>A (p.Asp645Glu) variant, the most frequent GAA pathogenic mutation in people of Southern Han Chinese ancestry, causes infantile-onset Pompe disease (IOPD), presenting neonatally with severe hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, profound muscle hypotonia, respiratory failure, and infantile mortality.

claims.text: Homozygous GAA c.1935C>A patient fibroblasts show extremely low GAA enzyme activity (0.08–0.82% of normal range for controls), consistent with IOPD.

citations.quote: This aligns with observed levels of low GAA enzyme activity (0.08–0.82% of normal range for control) previously measured in homozygous GAA c.1935C>A patient fibroblasts

claims.text: A C2C12 myoblast cell line carrying the Gaa c.1935C>A mutation generated by CRISPR-Cas9 HDR showed near-abolished GAA enzymatic activity (~2.3% of wild-type) and increased glycogen accumulation (PAS staining), demonstrating a molecular and biochemical phenotype consistent with Pompe disease.

citations.quote: GAA enzymatic activity was almost abolished in Gaa c.1935C>A KI cells relative to Gaawt cells; less than 2.3% of WT GAA activity was detected in the KI cell line

claims.text: Homozygous Gaa em1935C>A knock-in mice had near-abolished GAA enzymatic activity (~1% of WT levels) in heart, skeletal muscle, and brain tissue, with normal Gaa mRNA expression levels, indicating the c.1935C>A missense mutation does not cause nonsense-mediated decay.

citations.quote: KI mice had normal Gaa mRNA levels with significantly reduced level of GAA hydrolysis activity (about 1% of WT) in heart and skeletal muscle, as well as brain tissue.

claims.text: Homozygous Gaa em1935C>A mice exhibited markedly increased lysosomal glycogen storage in heart, diaphragm, and gastrocnemius muscle tissue, but only a slight (non-significant) increase in brain glycogen, in contrast to knock-out mice which showed significant glycogen increase in all tissues including brain.

citations.quote: Compared to the unaffected WT or HET groups, KI and KO mice had abnormally elevated lysosomal glycogen storage in heart, diaphragm, and gastrocnemius muscle tissue. Interestingly, increased glycogen storage in whole-brain homogenates was observed in KO mice, but not in KI mice, which had a slight, but not statistically significant, increase in glycogen load

claims.text: Three-month-old homozygous Gaa em1935C>A mice demonstrated hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (significantly increased IVSd, LVPWd, and LVMI) and skeletal muscle weakness (approximately 19% reduction in normalized forelimb grip strength in males), but no premature mortality.

citations.quote: Increases in interventricular septal diameter (IVSd), LV posterior wall diameter (LVPWd), and LV mass index (LVMI) were observed in KI mice, compared to WT and/or HET mice, indicating pronounced hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.
the male Gaaem1935C>A KI mouse showed a significant reduction (~19%) in normalized peak tension force compared to WT mice, indicating decreased forelimb muscle strength in KI mice

claims.text: Homozygous Gaa em1935C>A mice displayed impaired autophagy (increased LC3B-II/LC3B-I ratio) in skeletal muscles (diaphragm and gastrocnemius) and increased LAMP1 immunoreactivity in brain neuronal cell bodies, consistent with lysosomal storage burden.

citations.quote: The ratio of LC3B-II:LC3B-I is increased (Fig. 5D), demonstrating impaired autophagosome-lysosome fusion, in skeletal muscles (diaphragm and gastrocnemius) but not cardiac muscle of the KI model.

LAMP1 staining in the brain sections from 3-month-old WT and KI mice were examined in three representative areas of the brain (frontal cortex, hippocampus, and cerebellum), demonstrating markedly increased LAMP1 immunoreactivity in KI neuronal cell bodies compared to WT controls

claims.text: A possible founder effect for the c.1935C>A variant is suggested, stemming from a diaspora of Southern Han Chinese to Taiwan and other locations, based on a specific haplotype with conserved polymorphic markers.

citations.quote: occur in context of a specific haplotype with conserved polymorphic markers linked to Taiwanese Pompe IOPD patients comparing to normal individuals. This may suggest the existence of a founder effect stemming from a diaspora of Southern Han Chinese to Taiwan and other locations

claims.text: The Gaa em1935C>A knock-in mouse model does not show neonatal/infantile mortality despite near-zero GAA activity, which is a significant divergence from the human IOPD phenotype caused by this variant where neonatal death is the inevitable clinical outcome in untreated patients.

citations.quote: A significant divergence of the model from human GAA c.1935C>A IOPD is the lack of infantile mortality in KI mice.
neonatal death is the inevitable clinical outcome in untreated IOPD patients

claims.text: The GAA c.1935C>A variant is a missense (cytosine to adenine transversion) in exon 14 of the GAA gene, resulting in the amino acid substitution p.Asp645Glu (p.D645E), and does not affect GAA mRNA levels.

citations.quote: The missense Gaac.1935C>A mutation in exon 14 of the Gaa gene leads to an amino acid substitution; therefore, we did not expect any nonsense-mediated decay in Gaac.1935C>A mRNA transcripts.
